title: I Am Not the Christ
subtitle: John the Baptist confesses the truth before the priests and Levites — and turns the question on you

stillness: Be still, and let the question come to you.
Before you answer God, fall silent.
Ask yourself, in the quiet, who you are.
gospel: This is the testimony of John,
when the Jews sent priests and Levites from Jerusalem to him,
to ask him: Who are you?
And he confessed, and did not deny;
and he confessed: I am not the Christ.
And they asked him: What then? Are you Elijah?
And he said: I am not.
Are you the Prophet? And he answered: No.
behold: The priests and Levites are sent from Jerusalem; they stand before John and ask him, Who are you?
And this same question God asks of you. Before you answer, examine yourself first.
Mark the four questions to be set before everyone: what you are in nature, who you are in person, of what kind you are in your ways, how great you are in spiritual stature.

The first: who are you in nature? You are earth, by reason of the body — be humbled against pride. You are spirit, by reason of the soul — strain upward against greed. You are a rational creature — live reasonably against lust.

O proud one, you have unmade your nature: you are not earth that humbles itself, but air that lifts itself up; not spirit seeking the things of the spirit, but flesh that savors the things of earth; not a man using reason, but a beast living brutishly.
The second question is asked of you when you knock at the door, saying: Lord, Lord, open to us.
Perhaps you will answer: I am a Christian. But, as Ambrose says, it is a lie to call oneself a Christian and not to do the works of Christ.
Or perhaps you will answer: I am a friend. But hear what He says: You are my friends, if you do what I command you.
If you are neither, you will hear: Amen I say to you, I do not know you.
See John then, who though he was of such power and authority among the Jews that they believed him to be the Christ, was not lifted up into pride, nor seized a name and honor not his own.
consider: John confessed the truth, and did not deny it — for to deny it would be to deny Christ himself, who is the Truth.
He confessed himself not to be what he was not; he did not deny himself to be what he was.
He confessed he was not the Christ; he did not deny he was Christ's forerunner.
He confessed he was not the Bridegroom of the Church; he did not deny he was the friend.
He confessed he was not the Word; he did not deny he was the voice.
He chose to stand firmly within himself, lest he be emptily carried beyond himself by men's opinion.
Here is John's humility, than which nothing is greater.
As Chrysostom says: it belongs to the devoted servant not only to refuse to seize his master's glory, but even to reject it when many offer it.
This humility Lucifer did not have, who willed to seize for himself the glory of the Godhead.
This humility our first parents did not have, who willed to usurp the likeness of divine wisdom.
And even today some imitate Lucifer: tyrants, who rule by violence and cruelty; the ambitious, who wickedly exalt themselves to seek the higher place.
Some imitate the first man: heretics and men of worldly wisdom, who wish to know more than they ought to know.
Some prefigure Antichrist: hypocrites, feigning holiness, and liars and deceivers, denying the truth.
prayer: Lord, Lord, open to us, for we stand knocking at your door.
We dare not call ourselves yours if we do not do your works — for that, as your servant says, would be a lie.
You have said: You are my friends, if you do what I command you; do not say to us, I do not know you.
Give us the humility of John, than which nothing is greater,
who confessed he was not the Christ, and was not lifted up into pride.
Number us, who humbly acknowledge our own weakness, among the members of your body;
let us never be cut off from you by seizing a glory that is not ours.
Make us not air that lifts itself up, but earth that is humbled;
not the voice that claims to be the Word, but only a voice that prepares your way.

latinJewel.latin: Magis enim voluit infirmitatem suam humiliter recognoscendo inter membra Christi numerari, quam ejus nomen immerito usurpando, ab ipso Christi corpore abscindi.
latinJewel.english: For he chose rather, by humbly acknowledging his own weakness, to be numbered among the members of Christ, than, by usurping the name that was not his, to be cut off from the very body of Christ.
